title: Does anyone share bots to try here?
author: tauru_ai

comments.author: UnitedAcanthaceae118
comments.body:
Inb4 "if it worked they wouldn't be sharing it."

There are plenty of bots and strategies that make 5-15% per year but aren't valuable enough to keep secret. Maybe they have a lot of risk built-in or only work on certain high-risk markets. 

Question is: What kind of profit are you looking for? 100% per year? Unlikely to find it on a public forum.

15% per year? Plenty of public strategies that can potentially offer that, especially in high risk niches like crypto or options trading. 

I would not use bots though. I would use prompts. Bots lock you in to a specific way of doing things, and require code/servers/subscriptions. 

Prompts on the other hand are just text strings that you can reuse everywhere. 

The prompt workflow is surprisingly easy compared to bots:

* Find a decent prompt/strategy. There are plenty available for free on the internet. For example you can go on a platform like Everstrike and find 2-5 prompts that will probably work OK (won't make you a millionaire but can probably make you 10-20% per year with the right configuration). Refine them with Claude/ChatGPT.

* Run them in demo mode on a prompt trading platform for 1 week. Keep the ones that make a profit. Discard the ones that don't.

* Migrate to live. Use a very small amount of funds in the beginning. I like to test on small amounts like $50. Run in live for 1-2 weeks. If they are still profitable, increase the amount slowly.

replies.author: tauru_ai
replies.body:
Not at all. I’m not expecting for 100%
I’m thinking even about portfolio rebalancing 
Ai suggestions but something 

I really like the prompt strategy, but some solution are connected to data sources directly and I found it better than just asking Claude for example.

Do you think your prompt flow is covering all data sources to take a better decision for you?

replies.author: UnitedAcanthaceae118
replies.body:
Usually you can connect the data sources that are required.

For example on Everstrike (prompt trading platform) you can paste in a prompt and click "Enhance with AI". Then AI will select some data sources that it thinks your prompt will need and attach them to the prompt. Once the prompt is live it will get all the data that it needs. If you later feel that its missing some data you can manually add it via settings.

If you need extremely custom data, prompts may not work and you may need to use a custom bot instead. But for generic data sources like technical indicators and orderbook data prompts are definitely viable.

replies.author: tauru_ai
replies.body:
Can you define extremely custom data? 
Or better if you can explain where exactly do you find the bot helpful?
Long term? Are you care about time sensitivity?
Or maybe not will be better if you want “auto tracking” and optional “auto investing”?

comments.author: Top-Career-277
comments.body:
Ça arrive que des gens partagent des bots, mais honnêtement fais super attention avec ça.

La plupart du temps, soit c’est mal expliqué, soit c’est optimisé pour un contexte précis… soit carrément pas fiable. Un bot peut fonctionner à un moment et devenir mauvais dès que le marché change.

Si tu veux tester, le mieux c’est de :  
👉 comprendre comment il fonctionne  
👉 tester en démo ou avec un petit capital  
👉 éviter tout ce qui promet des gains “faciles”

Perso j’ai arrêté de chercher “le bon bot” et je me suis plutôt concentré sur le contexte marché (tendance, volatilité, etc.), parce que même le meilleur bot lancé au mauvais moment peut faire n’importe quoi.

C’est d’ailleurs pour ça que j’ai développé un petit outil 👉 [https://captentdb.fr](https://captentdb.fr)

L’idée c’est d’avoir une vision plus claire du marché avant d’utiliser un bot ou de prendre une position.

Donc oui tu peux tester, mais garde en tête que le bot c’est juste un outil — ce qui fait la différence, c’est comment et quand tu l’utilises 👍

comments.author: Expensive-Badger-142
comments.body: Yea, what kind of bots are you looking to try or have for others to try

replies.body:

On my side I’m looking for something which will bring an extra value, will actually know me. A little bit further than just a prompt

replies.author: Expensive-Badger-142
replies.body: Mhmm that’s unique, I would recommend promoting grok to look through git repos for this specific thing. When it finds it, fork it then take that forked project save to local machine. Then have Claude or Codex do exactly what you’re looking to do with it.

comments.author: FilmFreak1082
comments.body:
I built one. IOI - trades spot on Binance via API. Your funds stay on your own account, no deposits to third parties.

To address the "if it worked they wouldnt share it" point - I share it because theres a commission/subscription model. The bot makes money for users, I take a small cut. Incentives are aligned. If the bot stops performing I stop earning. Thats the whole business model.

Not looking for 100% per year. Last month the bot did about 2% during the worst week of the year while BTC dropped 7%. In good months its done up to 45%. The strategy isnt trying to hit home runs - it buys dips, sells recoveries, splits across multiple small positions. Thousands of trades per month, 92.7% win rate.

The prompt/strategy approach the other commenter mentioned is interesting but it still requires you to execute manually or build infrastructure to automate it. Mine is a mobile app - pick a pair, set a budget, press start. Built it for people who dont want to deal with code, servers, or prompts.

You can dry run it first with fake money - same algorithm, no risk. [getioi.app](http://getioi.app) (Android only for now).

Happy to answer any questions about the approach or the results.
